{{Infobox ancient site}}

[[File:Goshiki-zuka Kofun, zenpoubu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Akashi Strait|en|Akashi Strait|WD=Q845336|lt=Akashi Strait}} overlooking {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}})]]
'''{{ill|Coastal Zenpokoenfun|ja|海浜型前方後円墳|1=Coastal Zenpokoenfun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Coastal Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q116958143}}''' (海浜型前方後円墳) refers to a subset of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}}, the predominant Kofun type of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}, that were deliberately constructed at the tips of {{ill|Ria|en|Ria|WD=Q554394|lt=Ria coast}} formations, {{ill|Marine terrace|ja|海岸段丘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marine terrace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=marine terrace|WD=Q3518589}} edges, or {{ill|Beach ridge|en|Beach ridge|WD=Q2532557|lt=Beach ridge}} zones. These tombs were positioned along coastlines with excellent visibility from the sea. The concept was proposed by the archaeologist {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} between 2013 and 2015.{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Overview ==
It was previously recognized that some {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} were built facing the sea. However, during a 2013 symposium titled *The Age of Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun*, organized by the {{ill|Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|ja|かながわ考古学財団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|1=Kanagawa Archeology Foundation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30930414}}, {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} introduced the term "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" and further defined its characteristics. The symposium’s findings were later published as a general reference book in 2015.{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|qq=}}
{{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} outlined the following defining conditions for a Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun:{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=1-31|qq=}}
# The mound is constructed at a coastal location, typically on elevated ground, making it visible from the sea and situated at a key point along maritime transportation routes.
# These kofun are not located near [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] suitable for {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|lt=wet rice cultivation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}}, distinguishing them from inland Kofuns that were closely tied to agricultural economies.
# They are among the largest kofun in their respective regions. (This trend also applies to other mound shapes, such as large-scale round kofun.)
# They appear fully developed in the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}} (4th century), featuring tiered mound construction (段築構造), {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} rows (埴輪列), and [[Fukiishi]] (葺石).
# These kofun suddenly appear in regions with no previous history of large Kofuns. The ruling lineage associated with them typically lasts only one or two generations, meaning that either a single mound or, at most, two kofun are built before the lineage disappears. In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Notable Examples ==
* {{ill|Myōkensan Kofun|en|Myōkensan Kofun|WD=Q48763217|lt=Myōkensan Kofun}} ({{ill|Ōnishi, Imabari|ja|今治市大西町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi, Imabari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|en|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|WD=Q11607242|lt=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyōtango}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|en|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11653084|lt=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Zushi, Kanagawa|en|Zushi, Kanagawa|WD=Q230843|lt=Zushi, Kanagawa}} / {{ill|Hayama, Kanagawa|ja|三浦郡葉山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayama, Kanagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|ja|内裏塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|qq=}} ({{ill|Futtsu|en|Futtsu|WD=Q498023|lt=Futtsu, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|lt=Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|Ichihara, Chiba|WD=Q207881|lt=Ichihara, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Isohama Kofun Cluster|en|Isohama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q60990014|lt=Isohama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja|東茨城郡大洗町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}})

== Overview ==
It was previously recognized that some {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} were built facing the sea. However, during a 2013 symposium titled *The Age of Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun*, organized by the {{ill|Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|ja|かながわ考古学財団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|1=Kanagawa Archeology Foundation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30930414}}, {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} introduced the term "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" and further defined its characteristics. The symposium’s findings were later published as a general reference book in 2015.{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|qq=}}
{{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} outlined the following defining conditions for a Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun:{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=1-31|qq=}}
# The mound is constructed at a coastal location, typically on elevated ground, making it visible from the sea and situated at a key point along maritime transportation routes.
# These kofun are not located near [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] suitable for {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|lt=wet rice cultivation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}}, distinguishing them from inland Kofuns that were closely tied to agricultural economies.
# They are among the largest kofun in their respective regions. (This trend also applies to other mound shapes, such as large-scale round kofun.)
# They appear fully developed in the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}} (4th century), featuring tiered mound construction (段築構造), {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} rows (埴輪列), and [[Fukiishi]] (葺石).
# These kofun suddenly appear in regions with no previous history of large Kofuns. The ruling lineage associated with them typically lasts only one or two generations, meaning that either a single mound or, at most, two kofun are built before the lineage disappears. In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Notable Examples ==

* {{ill|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|en|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|WD=Q11607242|lt=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyōtango}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|en|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11653084|lt=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Zushi, Kanagawa|en|Zushi, Kanagawa|WD=Q230843|lt=Zushi, Kanagawa}} / {{ill|Hayama, Kanagawa|ja|三浦郡葉山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayama, Kanagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|ja|内裏塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|qq=}} ({{ill|Futtsu|en|Futtsu|WD=Q498023|lt=Futtsu, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|lt=Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|Ichihara, Chiba|WD=Q207881|lt=Ichihara, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Isohama Kofun Cluster|en|Isohama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q60990014|lt=Isohama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja|東茨城郡大洗町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture}})

'''{{ill|Coastal Zenpokoenfun|ja|海浜型前方後円墳|1=Coastal Zenpokoenfun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Coastal Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q116958143}}''' (海浜型前方後円墳) refers to a subset of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, the predominant Kofun type of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, that were deliberately constructed at the tips of {{ill|Ria|en|Ria|WD=Q554394|lt=Ria coast|1=Ria}} formations, {{ill|Marine terrace|ja|海岸段丘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marine terrace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Marine terrace|WD=Q3518589}} edges, or {{ill|Beach ridge|en|Beach ridge|WD=Q2532557|lt=Beach ridge|1=Beach ridge}} zones. These tombs were positioned along coastlines with excellent visibility from the sea. The concept was proposed by the archaeologist {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} between 2013 and 2015.{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Overview ==
It was previously recognized that some {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} were built facing the sea. However, during a 2013 symposium titled *The Age of Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun*, organized by the {{ill|Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|ja|かながわ考古学財団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|1=Kanagawa Archeology Foundation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30930414}}, {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} introduced the term "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" and further defined its characteristics. The symposium’s findings were later published as a general reference book in 2015.{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|qq=}}
{{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} outlined the following defining conditions for a Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun:{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=1-31|qq=}}
# The mound is constructed at a coastal location, typically on elevated ground, making it visible from the sea and situated at a key point along maritime transportation routes.
# These kofun are not located near [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] suitable for {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|lt=wet rice cultivation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}}, distinguishing them from inland Kofuns that were closely tied to agricultural economies.
# They are among the largest kofun in their respective regions. (This trend also applies to other mound shapes, such as large-scale round kofun.)
# They appear fully developed in the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} (4th century), featuring tiered mound construction (段築構造), {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} rows (埴輪列), and [[Fukiishi]] (葺石).
# These kofun suddenly appear in regions with no previous history of large Kofuns. The ruling lineage associated with them typically lasts only one or two generations, meaning that either a single mound or, at most, two kofun are built before the lineage disappears. In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}

# These kofun are not located near [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] suitable for {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|lt=wet rice cultivation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}}, distinguishing them from inland Kofuns that were closely tied to agricultural economies.
# They are among the largest kofun in their respective regions. (This trend also applies to other mound shapes, such as large-scale round kofun.)
# They appear fully developed in the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} (4th century), featuring tiered mound construction (段築構造), {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} rows (埴輪列), and [[Fukiishi]] (葺石).
# These kofun suddenly appear in regions with no previous history of large Kofuns. The ruling lineage associated with them typically lasts only one or two generations, meaning that either a single mound or, at most, two kofun are built before the lineage disappears. In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Notable Examples ==
* {{ill|Myōkensan Kofun|en|Myōkensan Kofun|WD=Q48763217|lt=Myōkensan Kofun|1=Myōkensan Kofun}} ({{ill|Ōnishi, Imabari|ja|今治市大西町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi, Imabari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}})

* {{ill|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|en|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|WD=Q11607242|lt=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|1=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyōtango|1=Kyōtango}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|en|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11653084|lt=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|1=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Zushi, Kanagawa|en|Zushi, Kanagawa|WD=Q230843|lt=Zushi, Kanagawa|1=Zushi, Kanagawa}} / {{ill|Hayama, Kanagawa|ja|三浦郡葉山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayama, Kanagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|ja|内裏塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|qq=}} ({{ill|Futtsu|en|Futtsu|WD=Q498023|lt=Futtsu, Chiba|1=Futtsu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|lt=Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|Ichihara, Chiba|WD=Q207881|lt=Ichihara, Chiba|1=Ichihara, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Isohama Kofun Cluster|en|Isohama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q60990014|lt=Isohama Kofun Cluster|1=Isohama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja|東茨城郡大洗町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:海浜型前方後円墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site}}

[[File:Goshiki-zuka Kofun, zenpoubu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Akashi Strait|en|Akashi Strait|WD=Q845336|lt=Akashi Strait|1=Akashi Strait}} overlooking {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}})]]
'''{{ill|Coastal Zenpokoenfun|ja|海浜型前方後円墳|1=Coastal Zenpokoenfun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Coastal Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q116958143}}''' (海浜型前方後円墳) refers to a subset of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, the predominant Kofun type of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, that were deliberately constructed at the tips of {{ill|Ria|en|Ria|WD=Q554394|lt=Ria coast|1=Ria}} formations, {{ill|Marine terrace|ja|海岸段丘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marine terrace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Marine terrace|WD=Q3518589}} edges, or {{ill|Beach ridge|en|Beach ridge|WD=Q2532557|lt=Beach ridge|1=Beach ridge}} zones. These tombs were positioned along coastlines with excellent visibility from the sea. The concept was proposed by the archaeologist {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} between 2013 and 2015.{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Overview ==
It was previously recognized that some {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} were built facing the sea. However, during a 2013 symposium titled *The Age of Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun*, organized by the {{ill|Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|ja|かながわ考古学財団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|1=Kanagawa Archeology Foundation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30930414}}, {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} introduced the term "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" and further defined its characteristics. The symposium’s findings were later published as a general reference book in 2015.{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|qq=}}
{{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} outlined the following defining conditions for a Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun:{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=1-31|qq=}}
# The mound is constructed at a coastal location, typically on elevated ground, making it visible from the sea and situated at a key point along maritime transportation routes.
# These kofun are not located near [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] suitable for {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|lt=wet rice cultivation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}}, distinguishing them from inland Kofuns that were closely tied to agricultural economies.
# They are among the largest kofun in their respective regions. (This trend also applies to other mound shapes, such as large-scale round kofun.)
# They appear fully developed in the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} (4th century), featuring tiered mound construction (段築構造), {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} rows (埴輪列), and [[Fukiishi]] (葺石).
# These kofun suddenly appear in regions with no previous history of large Kofuns. The ruling lineage associated with them typically lasts only one or two generations, meaning that either a single mound or, at most, two kofun are built before the lineage disappears. In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Notable Examples ==
* {{ill|Myōkensan Kofun|en|Myōkensan Kofun|WD=Q48763217|lt=Myōkensan Kofun|1=Myōkensan Kofun}} ({{ill|Ōnishi, Imabari|ja|今治市大西町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi, Imabari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|en|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|WD=Q11607242|lt=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|1=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyōtango|1=Kyōtango}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|en|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11653084|lt=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|1=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Zushi, Kanagawa|en|Zushi, Kanagawa|WD=Q230843|lt=Zushi, Kanagawa|1=Zushi, Kanagawa}} / {{ill|Hayama, Kanagawa|ja|三浦郡葉山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayama, Kanagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|ja|内裏塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|qq=}} ({{ill|Futtsu|en|Futtsu|WD=Q498023|lt=Futtsu, Chiba|1=Futtsu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|lt=Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|Ichihara, Chiba|WD=Q207881|lt=Ichihara, Chiba|1=Ichihara, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Isohama Kofun Cluster|en|Isohama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q60990014|lt=Isohama Kofun Cluster|1=Isohama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja|東茨城郡大洗町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}})

# They appear fully developed in the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} (4th century), featuring tiered mound construction (段築構造), {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} rows (埴輪列), and [[Fukiishi]] (葺石).
# These kofun suddenly appear in regions with no previous history of large Kofuns. The ruling lineage associated with them typically lasts only one or two generations, meaning that either a single mound or, at most, two kofun are built before the lineage disappears. In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Notable Examples ==

* {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|en|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|WD=Q11607242|lt=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|1=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyōtango|1=Kyōtango}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|en|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11653084|lt=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|1=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Zushi, Kanagawa|en|Zushi, Kanagawa|WD=Q230843|lt=Zushi, Kanagawa|1=Zushi, Kanagawa}} / {{ill|Hayama, Kanagawa|ja|三浦郡葉山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayama, Kanagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|ja|内裏塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|qq=}} ({{ill|Futtsu|en|Futtsu|WD=Q498023|lt=Futtsu, Chiba|1=Futtsu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|lt=Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|Ichihara, Chiba|WD=Q207881|lt=Ichihara, Chiba|1=Ichihara, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Isohama Kofun Cluster|en|Isohama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q60990014|lt=Isohama Kofun Cluster|1=Isohama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja|東茨城郡大洗町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:海浜型前方後円墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site}}

[[File:Goshiki-zuka Kofun, zenpoubu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Akashi Strait|en|Akashi Strait|WD=Q845336|lt=Akashi Strait|1=Akashi Strait}} overlooking {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}})]]
'''{{ill|Coastal Zenpokoenfun|ja|海浜型前方後円墳|1=Coastal Zenpokoenfun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Coastal Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q116958143}}''' (海浜型前方後円墳) refers to a subset of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, the predominant Kofun type of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, that were deliberately constructed at the tips of {{ill|Ria|en|Ria|WD=Q554394|lt=Ria coast|1=Ria}} formations, {{ill|Marine terrace|ja|海岸段丘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marine terrace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Marine terrace|WD=Q3518589}} edges, or {{ill|Beach ridge|en|Beach ridge|WD=Q2532557|lt=Beach ridge|1=Beach ridge}} zones. These tombs were positioned along coastlines with excellent visibility from the sea. The concept was proposed by the archaeologist {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} between 2013 and 2015.{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Overview ==
It was previously recognized that some {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} were built facing the sea. However, during a 2013 symposium titled *The Age of Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun*, organized by the {{ill|Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|ja|かながわ考古学財団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|1=Kanagawa Archeology Foundation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30930414}}, {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} introduced the term "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" and further defined its characteristics. The symposium’s findings were later published as a general reference book in 2015.{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|qq=}}
{{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} outlined the following defining conditions for a Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun:{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=1-31|qq=}}
# The mound is constructed at a coastal location, typically on elevated ground, making it visible from the sea and situated at a key point along maritime transportation routes.
# These kofun are not located near [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] suitable for {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|lt=wet rice cultivation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}}, distinguishing them from inland Kofuns that were closely tied to agricultural economies.
# They are among the largest kofun in their respective regions. (This trend also applies to other mound shapes, such as large-scale round kofun.)
# They appear fully developed in the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} (4th century), featuring tiered mound construction (段築構造), {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} rows (埴輪列), and [[Fukiishi]] (葺石).
# These kofun suddenly appear in regions with no previous history of large Kofuns. The ruling lineage associated with them typically lasts only one or two generations, meaning that either a single mound or, at most, two kofun are built before the lineage disappears. In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Notable Examples ==
* {{ill|Myōkensan Kofun|en|Myōkensan Kofun|WD=Q48763217|lt=Myōkensan Kofun|1=Myōkensan Kofun}} ({{ill|Ōnishi, Imabari|ja|今治市大西町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi, Imabari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|WD=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|en|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|WD=Q11607242|lt=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|1=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyōtango|1=Kyōtango}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|en|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11653084|lt=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|1=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Zushi, Kanagawa|en|Zushi, Kanagawa|WD=Q230843|lt=Zushi, Kanagawa|1=Zushi, Kanagawa}} / {{ill|Hayama, Kanagawa|ja|三浦郡葉山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayama, Kanagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|ja|内裏塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|qq=}} ({{ill|Futtsu|en|Futtsu|WD=Q498023|lt=Futtsu, Chiba|1=Futtsu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|lt=Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|Ichihara, Chiba|WD=Q207881|lt=Ichihara, Chiba|1=Ichihara, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Isohama Kofun Cluster|en|Isohama Kofun Cluster|WD=Q60990014|lt=Isohama Kofun Cluster|1=Isohama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja|東茨城郡大洗町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}})

In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Notable Examples ==
* {{ill|Myōkensan Kofun|en|Myōkensan Kofun|qid=Q48763217|lt=Myōkensan Kofun|1=Myōkensan Kofun}} ({{ill|Ōnishi, Imabari|ja|今治市大西町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi, Imabari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|qid=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|qid=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|en|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|qid=Q11607242|lt=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|1=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|qid=Q513897|lt=Kyōtango|1=Kyōtango}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|en|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11653084|lt=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|1=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Zushi, Kanagawa|en|Zushi, Kanagawa|qid=Q230843|lt=Zushi, Kanagawa|1=Zushi, Kanagawa}} / {{ill|Hayama, Kanagawa|ja|三浦郡葉山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayama, Kanagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|ja|内裏塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|qq=}} ({{ill|Futtsu|en|Futtsu|qid=Q498023|lt=Futtsu, Chiba|1=Futtsu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|lt=Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|Ichihara, Chiba|qid=Q207881|lt=Ichihara, Chiba|1=Ichihara, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Isohama Kofun Cluster|en|Isohama Kofun Cluster|qid=Q60990014|lt=Isohama Kofun Cluster|1=Isohama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja|東茨城郡大洗町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}})

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:海浜型前方後円墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site}}

[[File:Goshiki-zuka Kofun, zenpoubu-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Akashi Strait|en|Akashi Strait|qid=Q845336|lt=Akashi Strait|1=Akashi Strait}} overlooking {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|qid=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}})]]
'''{{ill|Coastal Zenpokoenfun|ja|海浜型前方後円墳|1=Coastal Zenpokoenfun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Coastal Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q116958143}}''' (海浜型前方後円墳) refers to a subset of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, the predominant Kofun type of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, that were deliberately constructed at the tips of {{ill|Ria|en|Ria|qid=Q554394|lt=Ria coast|1=Ria}} formations, {{ill|Marine terrace|ja|海岸段丘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marine terrace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Marine terrace|qid=Q3518589}} edges, or {{ill|Beach ridge|en|Beach ridge|qid=Q2532557|lt=Beach ridge|1=Beach ridge}} zones. These tombs were positioned along coastlines with excellent visibility from the sea. The concept was proposed by the archaeologist {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11485567}} between 2013 and 2015.{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Overview ==
It was previously recognized that some {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} were built facing the sea. However, during a 2013 symposium titled *The Age of Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun*, organized by the {{ill|Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|ja|かながわ考古学財団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Archaeology Foundation|1=Kanagawa Archeology Foundation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30930414}}, {{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11485567}} introduced the term "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" and further defined its characteristics. The symposium’s findings were later published as a general reference book in 2015.{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|qq=}}
{{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11485567}} outlined the following defining conditions for a Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun:{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=1-31|qq=}}
# The mound is constructed at a coastal location, typically on elevated ground, making it visible from the sea and situated at a key point along maritime transportation routes.
# These kofun are not located near [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] suitable for {{ill|Rice Cultivation|ja|稲作|lt=wet rice cultivation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rice Cultivation|qq=}}, distinguishing them from inland Kofuns that were closely tied to agricultural economies.
# They are among the largest kofun in their respective regions. (This trend also applies to other mound shapes, such as large-scale round kofun.)
# They appear fully developed in the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} (4th century), featuring tiered mound construction (段築構造), {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} rows (埴輪列), and [[Fukiishi]] (葺石).
# These kofun suddenly appear in regions with no previous history of large Kofuns. The ruling lineage associated with them typically lasts only one or two generations, meaning that either a single mound or, at most, two kofun are built before the lineage disappears. In some cases, there is a gap in time before a new ruling lineage emerges.
# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
== Notable Examples ==
* {{ill|Myōkensan Kofun|en|Myōkensan Kofun|qid=Q48763217|lt=Myōkensan Kofun|1=Myōkensan Kofun}} ({{ill|Ōnishi, Imabari|ja|今治市大西町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi, Imabari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|qid=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|qid=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} ({{ill|Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja|神戸市垂水区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarumi-ku, Kobe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|en|Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|qid=Q11607242|lt=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun|1=Amino Chōshiyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|qid=Q513897|lt=Kyōtango|1=Kyōtango}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|en|Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11653084|lt=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster|1=Nagae-Sakurayama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Zushi, Kanagawa|en|Zushi, Kanagawa|qid=Q230843|lt=Zushi, Kanagawa|1=Zushi, Kanagawa}} / {{ill|Hayama, Kanagawa|ja|三浦郡葉山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayama, Kanagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|ja|内裏塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dairizuka Kofun Cluster|qq=}} ({{ill|Futtsu|en|Futtsu|qid=Q498023|lt=Futtsu, Chiba|1=Futtsu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|lt=Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|Ichihara, Chiba|qid=Q207881|lt=Ichihara, Chiba|1=Ichihara, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}})
* {{ill|Isohama Kofun Cluster|en|Isohama Kofun Cluster|qid=Q60990014|lt=Isohama Kofun Cluster|1=Isohama Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja|東茨城郡大洗町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōarai, Ibaraki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}})

{{ill|Hirose Kazuo|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Kazuo|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11485567}} outlined the following defining conditions for a Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun:{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=1-31|qq=}}

# They exhibit regional clustering, with prominent examples found along Japan’s coastline.
In Western Japan, these kofun appear along the eastern and western coasts of Kyushu (九州), the Seto Inland Sea (瀬戸内海), and Osaka Bay (大阪湾). In Eastern Japan, they are found along Tokyo Bay (東京湾), the former Katori Sea (香取海), and the Pacific coastline of the Tōhoku region (東北地方). Given their high visibility from the sea and strategic locations near inlets and capes, these tombs are thought to have belonged to rulers who controlled maritime trade and navigation. Additionally, because they often appear in regions without a previous tradition of mound-building and vanish quickly, it is speculated that their construction was heavily influenced by the central authority of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato state|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権).{{Sfn|Hirose|2015|pp=21-25|qq=}}
The archaeologist {{ill|Nishikawa Shūichi|ja|西川修一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikawa Shūichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} notes that the concept of "Coastal Keyhole-shaped Kofun" is more specific than broader terms like "Marine Kofun" (海の古墳), which includes other types of tombs such as box-shaped stone cist tombs (箱式石棺墓).{{Sfn|Nishikawa|2018|p=3|qq=}}
